chapter: Longevity Effects of Restricting Calories and Fasting

claims.claim: Consuming only non-starchy vegetables on fasting days powerfully shuts down the insulin/IGF-1/mTOR pro-ageing pathway
claims.mechanism: Absence of animal protein, grains and fruit combined with very low calorie content (~500 cal/day) inhibits the insulin/IGF-1/mTOR pathway that modulates ageing and cancer development
claims.study_ref: Refs 46, 47 in book; author's own clinical studies

claims.claim: Vegetable fasting 2-3 times per week causes significant weight loss without calorie counting
claims.mechanism: Very low calorie density of non-starchy vegetables keeps intake below 500 cal/day, achieving ~20-23% weekly calorie restriction

claims.claim: Vegetable fasting activates longevity pathways via hormesis
claims.mechanism: Fasting acts as chronic mild stressor (hormetic agent) that activates cellular protective and anti-ageing pathways, increases stress resistance, elevates cortisol moderately, and enhances removal of damaged proteins
BDNF promotes growth and survival of nerve cells, strengthening brain function

consumption.amount: Unrestricted non-starchy vegetables, raw or cooked, keeping total daily intake under ~500 calories
consumption.frequency: 2-3 times per week (depending on baseline weight and metabolic profile)
consumption.preparation: Raw or cooked, dressed with 1 tablespoon extra-virgin olive oil, lemon juice or vinegar at lunch and dinner. No more than 2 tablespoons oil total per fasting day
consumption.reasoning: Author's clinical protocol for intermittent vegetable fasting; superior to 5:2 diet because excluding animal protein, grains and fruit maximises mTOR pathway suppression

relationships.description: Olive oil used as dressing on fasting days; each tablespoon adds ~100 calories so limited to 2 per day
Used as dressing alongside olive oil on fasting day vegetables

claims.claim: Suitable fat source during fasting days that does not disrupt fasting metabolic benefits
claims.mechanism: Provides essential fatty acids without activating insulin/IGF-1/mTOR pathway as animal proteins would

claims.claim: Slows absorption of carbohydrates when added to meals
claims.mechanism: Fat delays gastric emptying and carbohydrate absorption in the intestine

consumption.amount: 1 tablespoon per meal on fasting days (~100 cal per tablespoon); max 2 tablespoons per fasting day
consumption.reasoning: Primary fat source in the author's fasting and Mediterranean-style protocol

relationships.description: Used to dress vegetables on fasting days

claims.claim: Part of a high-quality diet that substituting for refined foods causes weight loss even without calorie counting
claims.mechanism: High fibre content induces early satiety via slower gastric emptying and small bowel transit; requires overfeeding by 200-300 cal/day to prevent unintended weight loss
claims.study_ref: Author's metabolic kitchen study; Ref 41 in book (Mediterranean diet study)

consumption.amount: Not precisely specified; fibre intake of 45g/day (3x Western average) in author's study diet
consumption.preparation: Whole-wheat bread, oat porridge, brown rice and similar minimally processed forms
consumption.reasoning: Core component of high-quality diet used in author's clinical studies

relationships.description: Combined in the author's study diet for complete nutrition

claims.claim: Part of fibre-rich diet that causes spontaneous weight loss and metabolic improvement
claims.mechanism: High fibre induces early satiety; source of plant protein, vitamins, phytocompounds and minerals

consumption.reasoning: Key component of the Mediterranean-style study diet

relationships.description: Combined with whole grains for complete protein and maximum fibre

claims.claim: Acceptable animal protein source in a longevity-focused diet when consumed moderately
claims.mechanism: Provides essential amino acids and omega-3 fatty acids without the same negative metabolic profile as red meat

consumption.preparation: Not specified (part of study diet protocol)
consumption.reasoning: Included in the author's calorie restriction study diet as primary animal protein source

relationships.description: Fish consumed 3x/week vs poultry only 1x/week in the study protocol

claims.claim: Acceptable animal protein when consumed sparingly in a longevity diet
claims.mechanism: Lower in harmful saturated fat and metabolic disruptors than red meat, but still activates insulin/IGF-1 pathway so must be limited

consumption.reasoning: Severely limited in study protocol; calorie-restricted practitioners occasionally consume pastured raised organic poultry

relationships.description: Fish preferred over poultry (3x vs 1x per week)

claims.claim: Suitable breakfast component in a high-quality longevity diet
claims.mechanism: Provides protein and probiotics without excessive saturated fat

consumption.reasoning: Included in the breakfast protocol of the author's clinical study

relationships.description: Combined at breakfast with oat porridge, nuts and fruit

claims.claim: Suitable whole grain breakfast food in a longevity diet
claims.mechanism: Provides slow-releasing carbohydrates and soluble fibre for sustained energy and satiety

consumption.reasoning: Included in breakfast protocol of clinical study

relationships.description: Combined at breakfast with yogurt, nuts and fruit

claims.claim: Far lower calorie density than cheese while providing fibre and protein
claims.mechanism: 100g cooked chickpeas = 164 cal (2.6g fat, 27g carbs incl 7g fibre, 9g protein) vs 100g Parmesan = 384 cal (28g fat, 0g fibre, 33g protein); fibre induces early satiety

consumption.reasoning: Used as example of how plant foods provide far more fibre and fewer calories per gram than animal products

relationships.description: Chickpeas presented as healthier alternative to calorie-dense, fibreless Parmesan cheese

claims.claim: Very high calorie density with zero fibre makes it a poor choice for weight management
claims.mechanism: 100g contains 384 calories, 28g fat, 0g carbohydrates, 0g fibre; no fibre means no satiety signalling from gut

consumption.reasoning: Used as negative example of calorie-dense, fibre-free food

relationships.description: Cheese shown as inferior to chickpeas for longevity diet

claims.claim: Induces greater satiety than white rice, making overeating difficult
claims.mechanism: Fibre-rich cell wall slows gastric emptying and small bowel transit, triggering early satiety signals

consumption.reasoning: Presented as example of how whole grain versions of foods naturally limit calorie intake

relationships.description: Brown rice recommended as replacement for white rice

ingredients.name: Refined/Processed Foods (sweets, sugary drinks, white bread, white rice, canned/pre-packaged foods)

claims.claim: Consumption on non-fasting days negatively influences metabolic response to fasting-induced weight loss
claims.mechanism: Disrupts gut microbiome composition and metabolite production, affecting metabolic health and increasing risk of cancer, inflammatory and autoimmune disorders
claims.study_ref: Author's preliminary data from ongoing experiment

claims.claim: Consuming refined foods during 16:8 time-restricted feeding causes huge increase in blood cholesterol
claims.mechanism: Refined foods (hamburgers, French fries) spike insulin and deliver trans fats and refined carbs that raise cholesterol even within a restricted feeding window

consumption.reasoning: All calorie-restricted practitioners in the author's studies have completely eliminated refined/processed foods; quality of food is paramount even during fasting protocols

relationships.description: Must be replaced with whole grains and other minimally processed foods

claims.claim: Traditional frugal food consumed at dusk during Ramadan fasting
claims.mechanism: Provides quick glucose replenishment after extended fast without large caloric load

consumption.reasoning: Referenced as traditional Ramadan practice of extreme time-restricted fasting
